picks.name: Broadcom Inc.
picks.rationale: Broadcom delivered record Q2 FY2026 results with revenue of $22.2B (+48% YoY) and AI semiconductor revenue surging +143% YoY, beating consensus with a 6.67% EPS surprise. The stock is consolidating in the $378–$390 range post-earnings — a healthy digestion of a massive fundamental beat — and Morgan Stanley reiterated AVGO as a 'Core AI Winner' on July 14. With 48 analysts at a consensus Strong Buy and a 12-month target of $523.73 (~35% upside), the custom ASIC and networking silicon franchise tied to hyperscaler multi-year commitments makes this the most durable AI infrastructure compounder in the portfolio.
picks.catalyst: Q3 FY2026 earnings (expected ~September 2026) with continued AI ASIC order momentum and VMware synergy flow-through to FCF margins
picks.entryNote: Consolidating $378–$390 post-Q2 beat; 12M analyst target of $523.73 implies 35%+ upside; ~30x fwd PE premium justified by 65%+ gross margins and elite ROIC; Morgan Stanley Buy reiterated July 14
picks.exitTrigger: Hyperscaler custom ASIC cancellations or meaningful slowdown in AI semiconductor revenue growth below 60% YoY
picks.keyRisk: Customer concentration — Apple and three hyperscalers represent the bulk of ASIC demand; any capex pause triggers multiple compression

picks.name: Alphabet Inc.
picks.rationale: GOOGL is reporting Q2 2026 earnings today (July 22) and early analyst commentary calls Google Cloud a 'massive beat' — the most important number per pre-earnings consensus. Q1 2026 already showed 22% revenue growth to $109.9B, net income +81% YoY to $62.6B, and Google Cloud accelerating to +48% YoY in Q4 2025. The stock is pulling back intraday on higher capex guidance (~$45B Q2 capex), but Berkshire/Abel's $31.6B stake at 9.2% ownership and the structural Search + Cloud compounding machine make any capex-driven weakness a high-conviction entry. GOOGL trades at ~25x forward PE — reasonable for a business generating $75B+ FCF annually.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings call today — Google Cloud beat confirmation and full-year capex/guidance reset establishing a new earnings baseline
picks.catalystWindow: Immediate — today through next 48 hours for post-earnings price discovery
picks.entryNote: Trading ~25x fwd PE vs. 5Y avg of ~23x; stock sinking intraday on capex hike — classic buy-the-dip on a high-quality compounder; Berkshire built $31.6B stake signaling conviction at these levels
picks.exitTrigger: Google Cloud growth decelerates below 30% YoY for two consecutive quarters, or Search market share loss to AI-native competitors exceeds 3 percentage points
picks.keyRisk: Capex overhang — if $180–190B full-year 2026 capex guidance is raised further, market may re-rate the stock lower on ROI skepticism before monetization catches up

picks.name: Micron Technology Inc.
picks.rationale: Micron's +228% YTD return reflects a genuine HBM memory supercycle: non-GAAP gross margins expanded from 23.7% in FY2024 to 40.9%, and revenue + earnings growth are both expected to exceed 100% for FY2026 ending August. Morgan Stanley on July 18 forecast a 25% rise in memory prices — a direct, near-term catalyst for Micron's next earnings print. The four major hyperscalers raising 2026 AI capex budgets to $750B ensures HBM demand remains structurally elevated, and DRAM pricing momentum has shifted decisively. Position is trimmed from prior weeks' #3 ranking to reflect extension risk, but the thesis is fully intact.
picks.catalyst: FY2026 Q4 earnings (expected late September) with memory pricing and HBM3E ramp confirmation; Morgan Stanley's July 18 25%-memory-price-rise forecast is a pre-earnings tailwind
picks.entryNote: Stock still extended YTD (+228%) but Morgan Stanley memory price forecast and hyperscaler capex acceleration justify holding; trim on strength above prior highs, accumulate on any 10%+ pullback
picks.exitTrigger: DRAM/HBM spot prices decline more than 15% QoQ or hyperscaler AI capex guidance is cut by any major cloud provider
picks.keyRisk: Memory cycles are notoriously volatile — an inventory glut signal from TSMC or Samsung would collapse the pricing thesis quickly

picks.name: Meta Platforms Inc.
picks.rationale: META is the only stock with 100% Tiger Cub consensus ownership and a 6.21% average portfolio weight — the clearest smart-money crowning in this universe. Q2 2026 earnings are due July 29, one week out, and Meta's custom silicon pivot (eliminating dependence on NVDA for inference) is compressing AI costs while expanding ad targeting precision. The ad engine's flywheel — 3B+ daily users, Advantage+ AI bidding, Reels monetization — generates best-in-class FCF per impression. ARK reduced but Tiger, Berkshire-equivalent quality mandates, and Ackman-adjacent consensus all point the same direction.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings July 29 — guidance on AI-driven ad revenue acceleration, Llama 4 deployment cost savings, and capex trajectory vs. Oracle's negative precedent
picks.entryNote: Forward PE ~23-25x on consensus 2026 EPS; 100% Tiger Cub consensus ownership with 6.21% avg weight is the highest smart-money conviction signal in the universe; near-term earnings event is binary but bias is positive given ad revenue re-acceleration
picks.exitTrigger: DAU growth stalls below 1% QoQ in core Facebook/Instagram or regulatory forced divestiture of Instagram advances materially
picks.keyRisk: Capex escalation — if Meta follows Oracle's playbook of accelerating spending without near-term revenue offset, the stock could re-price lower on ROI skepticism, as it did when GOOGL hiked capex today

picks.name: Goldman Sachs Group Inc.
picks.rationale: Goldman Sachs is the highest-conviction expression of the capital markets and M&A upcycle thesis: with the Fed holding rates at 3.5–3.75%, net interest margins are strong, and the broadening rotation from mega-cap tech into cyclicals is driving the M&A and IPO pipeline that GS leads. Q2 2026 banking earnings showed strong capital markets revenue, and the forward PE of ~13-14x vs. 12-13x historical median offers a thin but real value cushion. The HALO rotation — Heavy Assets, Low Obsolescence — that is gaining structural support in July directly benefits financial-sector incumbents with physical asset books and durable advisory moats.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings confirmation of investment banking revenue re-acceleration; sustained M&A and IPO pipeline visibility into H2 2026
picks.catalystWindow: Already reported; H2 2026 guidance reinforcement is the next leg
picks.entryNote: ~13-14x fwd PE vs. 12-13x historical median — near fair value but not expensive; NIM stability at 3.5-3.75% funds rate supports earnings floor; sector seeing confirmed July inflows per rotation data
picks.exitTrigger: HY OAS widens above 400bps (credit stress) or M&A deal flow collapses on a macro shock, compressing advisory revenue below Q4 2025 trough levels
picks.keyRisk: Credit cycle turn — if the 57K June payroll print signals the start of a genuine recession, credit losses and deal flow evaporate simultaneously

picks.name: S&P Global Inc.
picks.rationale: SPGI is the highest-quality compounder in Financials — a near-duopoly ratings business (with Moody's) that earns recurring revenue on every bond issuance, combined with Market Intelligence and Commodity Insights data franchises. In a late-cycle environment where credit issuance remains robust (HY OAS at 269bps encourages issuers to lock in spreads before any widening), SPGI's ratings revenue accelerates. The smart money brief flags SPGI as a 'quality compounder at reasonable multiples' — a specific institutional preference signal. ROIC exceeds 20%, FCF conversion is elite, and the business is structurally non-cyclical in its pricing power.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (expected late July) — bond issuance volume and Platts commodity data subscription growth are the key metrics; any credit re-rating cycle from HY to IG accelerates ratings revenue
picks.entryNote: Trades at ~30x fwd PE — premium but warranted for 8-10% EPS CAGR with 20%+ ROIC; smart money brief specifically names SPGI as a quality compounder institutional buy; thesis is rate-regime agnostic
picks.exitTrigger: Bond issuance volumes collapse more than 30% YoY (severe credit freeze) or DOJ successfully breaks up the SPGI-Moody's ratings duopoly
picks.keyRisk: Regulatory risk — the ratings agency duopoly is a perennial antitrust target; a surprise DOJ action would compress multiples immediately

picks.name: RTX Corporation
picks.rationale: RTX is the premier defense and aerospace compounder at the intersection of two secular multi-year demand drivers: defense modernization (NATO 2% GDP targets accelerating post-Ukraine) and commercial aerospace recovery (engine aftermarket revenues compounding as global air travel normalizes). The Middle East conflict and Iran war premium that the macro brief flags as an ongoing risk directly increases defense budget urgency across NATO allies — a structural revenue tailwind for RTX's Raytheon missile systems and Pratt & Whitney engines. XLI rose +20% H1 2026 and defense sub-sectors are identified as the highest-conviction longs within Industrials.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (expected late July) — Pratt & Whitney GTF engine retrofit revenue ramp and Raytheon missile system backlog growth are the key metrics vs. consensus
picks.entryNote: Trades at ~20-22x fwd PE, near Industrials sector median; defense backlog provides multi-year earnings visibility; geopolitical risk premium from Iran conflict directly benefits RTX's Raytheon segment
picks.exitTrigger: US defense budget cut exceeding 10% in next appropriations cycle, or Pratt & Whitney GTF engine recall expands to full fleet grounding with no insurance recovery
picks.keyRisk: GTF engine execution risk — ongoing geared turbofan inspections remain a cost and liability overhang; a broader fleet grounding would be a -15 to -20% event

picks.name: Quanta Services Inc.
picks.rationale: Quanta Services is the dominant electrical grid and pipeline infrastructure contractor in North America — the essential 'picks and shovels' play on AI power demand, grid electrification, and energy transition capex. Every hyperscaler data center requires transmission infrastructure that Quanta builds; every wind/solar installation requires grid interconnection that Quanta contracts. With XLI up +20% H1 2026 and the macro brief explicitly naming 'power equipment, cooling, grid hardening' as the first-dollar beneficiaries of the AI spending cycle, PWR's multi-year backlog visibility is unmatched in the Industrials sector.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July/early August) — backlog size and composition (renewable vs. grid hardening vs. data center interconnect) will indicate duration of the demand cycle
picks.entryNote: Trades at ~24-26x fwd PE — modest premium to Industrials median, fully justified by 15%+ revenue CAGR in backlog; grid electrification is a decade-long secular demand story not yet fully priced; no single customer represents more than 10% of revenue
picks.exitTrigger: Backlog growth decelerates to below 5% YoY or margin compression from labor cost inflation exceeds 200bps vs. guidance
picks.keyRisk: Labor market tightness for skilled electrical workers creates cost inflation and project delay risk in a sector where margins are already thin (~5-7% EBIT)

picks.name: Vistra Energy Corp.
picks.rationale: Vistra delivered Q1 2026 revenue of $5.6B (+43.4% YoY) with a 7.2% forward FCF yield — the highest on the momentum screen — and is directly positioned in nuclear and natural gas power generation that AI data centers are desperately seeking. The macro brief notes nuclear-related equities are posting sharp gains amid data center electricity demand, and Tepper's Appaloosa doubled VST exposure in Q1 2026. At ~12-14x fwd PE for a business generating exceptional FCF and growing earnings 40%+ YoY, VST is the rare momentum-plus-value-plus-quality combination that the late-cycle rotation into 'HALO' assets (Heavy Assets, Low Obsolescence) explicitly rewards.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (early August) — power price realizations, nuclear capacity factor, and data center contract pipeline are the key metrics; any new long-term PPA announcements with hyperscalers are immediate re-rating events
picks.entryNote: 7.2% fwd FCF yield at current price is exceptional for a growth business; ~12-14x fwd PE well below XLU average of 20x; Tepper doubled exposure in Q1; nuclear PPA contract announcements are the upside option
picks.exitTrigger: Nuclear plant capacity factor drops below 85% for two consecutive quarters (operational risk) or PJM/ERCOT power prices collapse more than 25% from current levels
picks.keyRisk: Power price volatility — VST is exposed to merchant power pricing in deregulated markets; a mild summer or surplus renewable generation can suppress power prices unexpectedly

picks.name: CrowdStrike Holdings Inc.
picks.rationale: CrowdStrike is up 63% YTD as of today's Cramer segment on CNBC's Mad Dash, where machine-on-machine AI cyberattacks were called a 'watershed moment' directly expanding CRWD's addressable demand. Net new ARR of $256M (+32% YoY) and FCF growth of 67% demonstrate the Falcon platform is operating as a security OS — with 51% of customers running 6+ modules, churn risk is minimal and ARPU expansion is structural. The 4-for-1 stock split completed July 2 has widened the retail investor base, and IBM's Q2 results today flagging rising enterprise cybersecurity urgency is a direct demand tailwind. Morgan Stanley and 40 of 52 covering analysts rate CRWD Buy.
picks.catalyst: FY2027 Q2 earnings (expected late August) — net new ARR growth rate vs. 32% benchmark and gross retention rate above 97% are the critical confirmation metrics
picks.entryNote: At $187.34 post-split, trades at ~164x fwd earnings — stretched but FCF yield is improving rapidly; 52-week range $139.57–$368.80 (pre-split adjusted); IBM cybersecurity comments today (July 22) are a same-day positive catalyst; weight reduced from prior weeks to reflect valuation
picks.exitTrigger: Net new ARR decelerates below 20% YoY for two consecutive quarters or a second major platform outage (following 2024 incident) triggers customer churn above 5%
picks.keyRisk: Valuation at 164x fwd earnings leaves no margin for error — any guidance miss or deceleration in ARR growth would trigger a rapid multiple de-rating of 20-30%

picks.name: SPDR Gold Shares ETF
picks.rationale: Gold is up ~9.8% YTD in 2026 and +129.5% since early 2024 — a structural multi-year bull market driven by central bank accumulation (90% of central banks cite crisis performance as a key reserve rationale), dollar debasement risk, and the ongoing Iran conflict's geopolitical risk premium. In the current late-cycle stagflationary regime — positive real rates but decelerating growth, sticky inflation, and a hawkish Fed — gold serves as the portfolio's primary left-tail hedge against both a fiscal/bond-market vigilante event (Tail Risk 3) and an Iran re-escalation (Tail Risk 2). The slight pullback from all-time highs near $4,037 provides a constructive entry vs. peak.
picks.catalyst: July 28-29 FOMC — any hawkish surprise or hike signal from Chair Warsh would be an immediate gold tailwind as real rates expectations adjust; Iran MOU framework sustainability is the ongoing geopolitical trigger
picks.entryNote: Consolidating from highs near $4,037; structurally supported by central bank demand floor; current entry below all-time high provides better risk/reward than chasing the peak; no credit risk, no earnings risk
picks.exitTrigger: Sustained Fed pivot to aggressive cutting (real rates deeply negative) combined with full Iran diplomatic resolution removing geopolitical premium — both conditions simultaneously would compress gold's risk-premium bid
picks.keyRisk: A surprise disinflationary shock (core PCE below 2.5%) could remove the stagflation premium and trigger a -10 to -15% gold drawdown as rate-cut expectations pull forward dollar strength

picks.name: Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF
picks.rationale: SCHD provides a 3.25% dividend yield with strict FCF and quality screens — its sector composition (Financials, Healthcare, Consumer Staples, Industrials) is explicitly underweight mega-cap tech, making it a structural hedge against the AI concentration risk that dominates 40%+ of the S&P 500 index weight. In the current rotation environment where institutions are moving into 'HALO' assets and dividend payers are attracting inflows, SCHD captures the quality-income thesis without single-name binary risk. The 3.25% yield meaningfully exceeds the equity risk premium on the current S&P 500 trailing earnings yield of 3.12%.
picks.catalyst: Continued late-cycle defensive rotation — any FOMC hawkish surprise or payroll deterioration below 57K accelerates institutional rotation into dividend quality names that SCHD owns
picks.catalystWindow: Ongoing; accelerates on any risk-off catalyst in next 1-3 months
picks.entryNote: 3.25% yield vs. 3.12% SPY trailing earnings yield — income advantage at lower risk; low fee ETF with quality/FCF screens; accumulate on market strength dips as it de-correlates from momentum names
picks.exitTrigger: Fed pivots to aggressive rate cuts triggering a violent rotation back into growth/momentum that causes SCHD to lag by more than 20 percentage points over two quarters — at which point risk-on redeployment is warranted
picks.keyRisk: In a strong bull market continuation (SPY +25%+), SCHD significantly underperforms and creates opportunity cost drag on the overall portfolio return

picks.name: Berkshire Hathaway Class B
picks.rationale: Berkshire's $397.4B cash hoard (59% of investable portfolio) and 14 consecutive quarters of net equity sales give Greg Abel maximum optionality to deploy at distressed prices in any risk-off event — making BRK.B the portfolio's all-weather fortress. Abel's $31.6B Alphabet stake signals strategic AI transformation while insurance, BNSF rail, and energy operating subsidiaries buffer against macro shocks. The combination of massive dry powder for opportunistic deployment, elite insurance underwriting discipline, and zero AI capex binary risk makes BRK.B unique in this universe. In a bear market scenario, Berkshire historically outperforms SPY by 15-20 percentage points.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 13F filing (~August 14) will reveal Abel's full positioning as of June 30 — including the Alphabet private placement confirmation and any new deployments from the $397B cash reserve
picks.entryNote: ~1.5x book value — reasonable for a conglomerate with $397B cash, consistent operating earnings, and 14 consecutive quarters of disciplined capital return; no individual name binary risk; near-zero blow-up probability by construction
picks.exitTrigger: Abel deploys the $397B cash reserve into a single leveraged acquisition at a peak-cycle valuation (the Buffett-era mistake to avoid), signaling the patience premium has been exhausted
picks.keyRisk: Opportunity cost — in a strong bull market, $397B sitting in T-bills earning 4.25% drags on total return vs. a fully invested equity portfolio; BRK.B has historically lagged in strong risk-on environments

picks.name: iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF
picks.rationale: IEF provides intermediate-duration Treasury exposure at the 7-10 year part of the curve where the 10-year yield sits at 4.18% — a 25-year-high real yield that provides genuine income and diversification vs. the equity book. Unlike TLT (which carries 20Y+ duration and maximum rate risk), IEF's 7-10Y duration is the sweet spot: enough sensitivity to benefit from any eventual Fed pivot while limiting the blow-up risk if yields re-test 4.7-5.0%. In a portfolio where the three dominant macro tail risks (AI capex reckoning, Iran re-escalation, bond vigilante event) all have distinct equity correlations, intermediate Treasuries are the cleanest uncorrelated hedge.
picks.catalyst: July 28-29 FOMC hold confirmation (already priced) followed by June PCE data July 30 — a sub-3.0% core PCE print would be the single largest positive catalyst for IEF as rate-cut expectations re-price September timing
picks.catalystWindow: July 29-30 — dual Fed/PCE risk events in back-to-back days
picks.entryNote: 10Y at 4.18% represents a 25-year real yield high; medium conviction reflects hawkish Fed uncertainty; sized at 4% to limit duration drag while maintaining the hedge function
picks.exitTrigger: 10-year yield breaks above 5.0% on sustained basis (bond vigilante event materializing) — at that point duration risk exceeds hedge benefit and cash is preferable
picks.keyRisk: Fed hikes or signals a hike at July 28-29 FOMC — would push the 10Y toward 4.7% and create a -4 to -6% drawdown in IEF immediately

picks.name: The Coca-Cola Company
picks.rationale: Coca-Cola is the portfolio's consumer staples anchor — a global pricing-power machine with 3%+ dividend yield, 60+ years of consecutive dividend increases, and revenues denominated in 200+ currencies providing natural diversification against dollar risk. In a late-cycle stagflationary regime where consumer discretionary is explicitly avoided (rate-cut dependent, facing 4.5%+ mortgage/card rates), KO's non-discretionary consumption pattern and brand equity make it the cleanest consumer defensive. The recent rotation into consumer staples (XLP up +5% YoY with institutional allocators considering overweight for 'durable earnings turnaround') directly supports this position.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (expected late July) — organic revenue growth vs. volume/price mix split; emerging market volume acceleration and pricing power in the face of tariff-driven input cost inflation are the critical metrics
picks.entryNote: Trades near 10-year median forward PE (~22-23x) with 3%+ dividend yield; Consumer Staples rotation has begun per institutional flow data; KO's brand moat and 200-currency revenue base provide the deepest defensive floor in the universe
picks.exitTrigger: Organic revenue growth decelerates to below 3% for two consecutive quarters while margin compression accelerates (input cost inflation exceeds pricing power) — signals the pricing moat is eroding
picks.keyRisk: GLP-1/Ozempic secular demand reduction — if weight-loss drugs structurally reduce caloric beverage consumption, KO's volume trajectory faces a multi-year headwind not yet reflected in consensus estimates

summary: The portfolio is constructed for a late-cycle stagflationary regime: restrictive real rates (+100bps above core CPI), a 3m10y yield curve that just re-inverted, 57K June payrolls, and a hawkish Fed holding at 3.50–3.75% with no cuts likely before September at earliest — all point toward quality-and-cash-flow discipline over speculative growth. The 10-name growth book concentrates in secular AI infrastructure winners (AVGO, GOOGL, MU, CRWD) with diversification into capital markets (GS, SPGI), defense/grid electrification Industrials (RTX, PWR), AI power (VST), and social/ad-tech (META) — a deliberate spread across five sectors that captures the 'what's working' themes from the macro brief while avoiding the rate-cut-dependent Consumer Discretionary and leveraged Small Cap traps. Today's GOOGL earnings (Google Cloud 'massive beat' per analyst commentary, stock sinking on capex hike) reinforce the thesis that AI infrastructure names offer the best entry points on capex-overhang-induced dips. All seven carry-overs from prior days (AVGO, GOOGL, META, GS, SPGI, RTX, PWR, VST, CRWD, MU) maintain intact theses — MU benefits from Morgan Stanley's July 18 forecast of 25% memory price increases; CRWD gains from IBM's July 22 cybersecurity urgency comments; the defensives (GLD, SCHD, BRK.B, IEF, KO) remain unchanged as the July 28–29 FOMC and July 30 PCE represent back-to-back binary risk events the defensive sleeve is specifically sized to absorb. The dominant risk being underwritten is an AI capex ROI reckoning — the most elevated tail risk at 35-45% probability — where hyperscaler capex announcements disappoint and trigger sector-wide multiple compression across AVGO, MU, and CRWD simultaneously.
diversificationNote: The growth book spans five GICS sectors: Information Technology (AVGO, MU, CRWD — three AI-infrastructure names with differentiated value-chain exposure: custom ASIC/networking, HBM memory, and endpoint cybersecurity), Communication Services (GOOGL, META — Cloud compounding and ad-tech monetization respectively, different revenue models), Financials (GS, SPGI — capital markets revenue and ratings/data duopoly, low correlation to semis), Industrials (RTX, PWR — defense backlog and grid electrification contractor, physical-asset HALO plays), and Utilities (VST — nuclear/gas power merchant, AI electricity demand). The defensive sleeve spans four exposures: gold commodity hedge (GLD), broad dividend/quality income ETF (SCHD), all-weather equity fortress with $397B cash (BRK.B), and intermediate-duration Treasuries (IEF) plus consumer staples anchor (KO) — no duplicate exposures across the five defensive slots.

picks.name: Broadcom Inc.
picks.rationale: AVGO delivered record Q2 2026 revenue of $22.2B (+48% YoY) with AI semiconductor sales surging 143% YoY, beating consensus, and guided Q3 to $29.4B — the strongest revenue acceleration since 2017. The Apple $30B+ custom chip deal through 2031, combined with multi-year XPU agreements with Google and Meta, structurally lock in multi-year revenue visibility that the momentum screen scores as a top-2 name. Morgan Stanley reaffirmed Buy this week calling AVGO 'a core AI winner,' and the 48-analyst consensus carries a Strong Buy with a $523 average price target — 41% above current levels near $370.
picks.catalyst: Q3 FY2026 earnings report (September 2, 2026) with $29.4B revenue guidance confirmation and AI semi revenue trajectory
picks.entryNote: Trading ~$370, consolidating in the $360-$410 range post-Q2 beat; above 200-day SMA. Forward P/E has re-rated lower from peak as share price digests the Q2 result — better entry than pre-June earnings. PEG well below 1.0 on 35%+ EPS growth estimates.
picks.exitTrigger: AI semiconductor revenue growth decelerates below 80% YoY, or Apple/Google/Meta signal custom ASIC budget freeze; gross margin compression below 65%
picks.keyRisk: Custom ASIC competition from in-house hyperscaler chip programs (Google TPU, Amazon Trainium) cannibalizing AVGO's XPU revenue stream

picks.name: Alphabet Inc.
picks.rationale: Berkshire Hathaway made its largest single-stock bet in a decade by nearly tripling its GOOGL stake to ~57.8M shares worth $16.6B — Greg Abel's highest-conviction AI platform call. Google Cloud is accelerating, advertising revenue is re-rated upward, and Waymo optionality remains unpriced. At ~$367 and ~20x forward P/E on a company growing EPS 20%+ with $100B+ in net cash, GOOGL is among the least extended mega-cap AI names with the widest moat in the cohort, making it the preferred platform-layer hold alongside AVGO.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings report (late July) — Google Cloud growth acceleration and AI revenue disclosure
picks.entryNote: Trading ~$367, forward P/E ~20x vs. 5-year avg ~25x; fortress balance sheet with $100B+ net cash. Technically consolidating above 50-day and 200-day SMAs. Berkshire accumulation provides institutional demand floor.
picks.exitTrigger: Google Cloud growth decelerates below 25% YoY, or Department of Justice search monopoly ruling results in forced structural remedies that impair advertising monetization
picks.keyRisk: DOJ antitrust ruling requiring search divestiture or search share loss to AI-native alternatives (Perplexity, ChatGPT Search) that structurally impairs the $200B+ advertising engine

picks.name: Goldman Sachs Group Inc.
picks.rationale: Goldman Sachs posted a blowout Q2 2026 EPS of $20.98 vs. the $14.51 estimate — a +44.6% beat, the largest positive EPS surprise in the Financials sector this quarter — driven by investment banking and capital markets reactivation. Unlike tech names re-rated on multiple expansion, GS re-rated on actual earnings beats; the momentum is fundamental and not yet crowded. The higher-for-longer rate environment, elevated capital markets activity, and the financial sector's Q2 revenue growth rate jumping to 12.4% from 8.9% create a durable mid-to-late cycle tailwind for the firm's franchise.
picks.catalyst: Q3 2026 earnings (mid-October) confirming sustained capital markets revenue and investment banking pipeline strength
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~13x forward P/E vs. 10-year median of ~13x — fairly valued, not extended. Fresh institutional accumulation post-Q2 beat. No crowding risk unlike AI tech names. Positive OBV trend initiated post-earnings.
picks.exitTrigger: Net revenues decline more than 15% QoQ due to capital markets seizure, or credit losses in lending book spike materially above guidance
picks.keyRisk: Capital markets activity collapses if the Fed hikes in October and freezes debt issuance/M&A activity, directly impacting GS advisory and underwriting revenue

picks.name: Meta Platforms Inc.
picks.rationale: Meta surged ~9% on July 1 after Bloomberg reported the company is building a cloud infrastructure business to sell excess AI compute, positioning it as a new rival to AWS and Azure — a potential re-rating event that moves META from a pure ad platform to an AI infrastructure and cloud compounder. Meta's custom silicon (MTIA) secures its bull case per TipRanks analysts this week, and with a multi-year Broadcom ASIC agreement locked, META's AI capex is building durable competitive infrastructure rather than renting capacity. Ad revenue remains the base, and the AI pivot is incremental upside.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — AI cloud business revenue disclosure and Llama monetization pathway
picks.entryNote: Forward P/E ~22x on a company with 20%+ revenue growth, $50B+ in annual FCF, and a new AI cloud option value. Technically strong — broke out July 1 on cloud news. Less valuation-extended than NVDA or AVGO.
picks.exitTrigger: AI cloud business generates less than $2B annual revenue by mid-2027, or core advertising revenue growth decelerates below 10% YoY signaling market share loss to TikTok/YouTube
picks.keyRisk: AI cloud build-out consumes $50B+ in incremental capex without commensurate revenue acceleration, destroying FCF yield and forcing a multiple de-rating

picks.name: RTX Corporation
picks.rationale: RTX is a uniquely positioned aerospace and defense compounder with near-term earnings catalysts (Q2 report July 23), a series of large NATO contract wins including a £2B UK training system award and $1.1B AIM-9X missile contract, and structural demand from a geopolitical environment that is durably repricing defense budgets higher. Jefferies reiterates Buy at $220 target and forecasts 2026 adjusted EPS of $7.10 vs. consensus $6.94, with a PEG ratio of 0.66 suggesting meaningful undervaluation relative to growth. The Hormuz conflict and NATO AMRAAM production expansion directly accelerate Raytheon's revenue and backlog.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings release on July 23 — Raytheon and Pratt & Whitney segment margin guidance update
picks.entryNote: Trading ~$195; PEG of 0.66 is historically attractive for a defense prime. Jefferies projects FCF of $8.6B vs. guidance midpoint of $8.5B. Citi issued Buy in late June. Quarterly dividend of $0.73/share declared. Technically consolidating above 200-day SMA.
picks.exitTrigger: Defense budget sequestration or a material resolution in the Hormuz/NATO conflict reduces missile demand outlook; Pratt & Whitney engine reliability issues escalate into program delays
picks.keyRisk: Bipartisan defense budget ceiling deal in late 2026 that reduces DoD procurement spending below current CBO baseline projections, impairing Raytheon's $60B+ backlog execution timeline

picks.name: S&P Global Inc.
picks.rationale: S&P Global completed the spin-off of its Mobility business on July 1, 2026, creating a leaner, higher-margin ratings and financial intelligence compounder — a classic post-spin re-rating setup. SPGI benefits directly from the higher-for-longer rate environment as debt issuance volumes and ratings activity remain elevated, while its Market Intelligence and Commodity Insights segments provide diversified recurring revenue. The higher-for-longer regime that pressures rate-sensitive sectors is a structural tailwind for SPGI's ratings franchise, which earns fees on every bond issued in a world of persistent Treasury supply.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — first post-Mobility spin-off report showing leaner margin profile and updated financial targets
picks.entryNote: Post-spin catalysts typically lead to 3-6 month outperformance as index inclusion rules adjust and the cleaner story attracts a new investor base. 20 Wall Street analysts cover with bullish consensus. picks.name: Quanta Services Inc.
picks.rationale: PWR is the leading pure-play contractor for grid infrastructure, transmission, and data center power connectivity — the physical backbone of AI buildout. The industrials sector data shows power-related businesses delivering ~35% sales growth and ~300% earnings growth, and Quanta's backlog growth of 33%+ positions it as a direct beneficiary of the AI-driven electricity demand supercycle. With the 10-year at 4.57%, utilities face headwinds, but PWR earns fees on building the grid rather than owning rate-regulated assets, insulating it from the rate-sensitive utility selloff.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (early August) — backlog update and power segment revenue acceleration
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~20x forward earnings, roughly in line with its historical range for a company with 15%+ organic growth. Backlogs are multi-year locked and provide earnings visibility. Technically constructive above 50-day SMA with strong institutional OBV.
picks.exitTrigger: Hyperscaler data center capex reduction causes a measurable decline in power interconnection project awards; or labor cost inflation compresses margins below 8% EBITDA
picks.keyRisk: A sudden pause in AI data center buildout — caused by a second DeepSeek-style efficiency shock — reduces near-term demand for new power infrastructure faster than the long-term grid upgrade cycle can compensate

picks.name: Vistra Corp.
picks.rationale: VST is the rare utility that functions as a growth stock — its deregulated power generation fleet (including nuclear) directly benefits from AI data center electricity demand, and David Tepper doubled his position (+114%) in Q1 2026 as part of his AI infrastructure buildout thesis. Unlike rate-regulated utilities crushed by the 30-year at 5.09%, VST sells power at market rates, benefiting from power price inflation driven by data center demand. The midstream/power sub-sector is the cleanest structural AI infrastructure story for 3-5 year holders per the sector rotation scorecard.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (August) — nuclear capacity factor and commercial power pricing realization versus forward curve
picks.entryNote: VST has pulled back from its 2026 highs alongside broader utility sector pressure but remains structurally differentiated by its deregulated model. Tepper's doubling at current-area prices provides smart money validation. Trades at a meaningful premium to regulated utility peers, justified by superior earnings growth.
picks.exitTrigger: ERCOT or PJM power prices collapse below $50/MWh on structural oversupply; or nuclear plant unexpected outages impair capacity factor below 85%
picks.keyRisk: Regulatory reclassification of nuclear plants or FERC intervention in power market pricing caps Vistra's ability to capture AI-driven electricity price premiums in deregulated markets

picks.name: CrowdStrike Holdings Inc.
picks.rationale: CrowdStrike is the secular leader in AI-native cybersecurity, operating in a market where defense spending is accelerating alongside geopolitical escalation from the Hormuz conflict and NATO expansion. CRWD's Falcon platform benefits from the same AI capex cycle that is powering AVGO and MSFT — enterprises deploying AI infrastructure must simultaneously harden security perimeters, creating a structural co-motion with AI buildout spending. Subscription revenue compound growth of 25%+ YoY, gross margins above 75%, and a beat rate of 4/4 in recent quarters validate the quality overlay.
picks.catalyst: Q2 FY2027 earnings (August) — ARR growth acceleration and platform module adoption expansion confirming post-July-2024-incident recovery is complete
picks.entryNote: CRWD has recovered from the 2024 software incident and re-established technical trend. Forward P/E elevated but consistent with 25%+ growth compounders with >75% gross margins. Cybersecurity sector inflows are accelerating alongside geopolitical risk premium expansion.
picks.exitTrigger: Annual Recurring Revenue growth decelerates below 20% YoY, signaling competitive erosion from Microsoft Security or SentinelOne; or customer churn rate rises above 5% post-incident

picks.name: Micron Technology Inc.
picks.rationale: Micron reached a $1 trillion market cap in May 2026 on HBM demand, posted a +22.55% EPS surprise ($25.11 vs. $20.49 estimate), and announced 16 Strategic Customer Agreements with binding take-or-pay commitments that de-risk its historically cyclical revenue profile. AI workloads are expected to consume ~70% of global high-end DRAM production by year-end 2026, structurally supporting memory pricing. The -20% drawdown over the past month alongside a broader chip selloff creates a 'fallen angel' accumulation opportunity for patient 2-5 year holders — acknowledging that near-term price momentum is broken while the fundamental earnings engine remains intact.
picks.catalyst: Q4 FY2026 earnings (late September) — HBM3E shipment ramp confirmation and Strategic Customer Agreement revenue recognition
picks.entryNote: Down ~20% from recent highs; SOXX entered bear market territory on July 17 per headline data. This is a phased accumulation entry — not a full position. The 16 take-or-pay SCAs provide a structural floor on demand visibility that prior semi cycles lacked. Accumulate in tranches on further weakness.
picks.exitTrigger: Strategic Customer Agreements are renegotiated at lower volumes or pricing, signaling HBM demand is peaking; or China export restrictions on HBM chip sales materially impair addressable market
picks.keyRisk: A second AI efficiency breakthrough (analogous to DeepSeek) reduces the memory-intensity of next-generation model training, causing a demand air pocket before the SCA commitments fully absorb supply

picks.name: SPDR Gold Shares ETF
picks.rationale: GLD is the portfolio's primary hedge against the stagflationary tail risk that dominates the macro briefing: the Fed projects headline PCE at 3.6% through 2026 with a return to 2% pushed to 2028, the 10-year trades at 4.57%, and the Hormuz ceasefire remains fragile. Gold corrected ~25% from its January 2026 all-time high of $5,300/oz on Fed rate-hike fears — creating a materially improved entry vs. earlier 2026 levels. With the real policy rate only +30bp above neutral and the equity risk premium negative, gold's role as a stagflation and USD debasement hedge is structurally intact.
picks.catalyst: Any Hormuz re-escalation, hot CPI print (≥4.5%), or Fed hike signal at the July 28-29 FOMC meeting re-ignites gold's safe-haven bid
picks.entryNote: GLD trading at $365, well off the ~$509 52-week high — a ~25-28% tactical flush on Fed hike fears. 1-year return vs. TLT still +18.76% vs. +2.34%. The correction is policy-fear driven, not a structural demand break. Entry here offers asymmetric upside if inflation persists.
picks.exitTrigger: Core PCE sustainably breaks below 2.5% for two consecutive readings, removing the stagflation premium; or USD strengthens more than 8% from current levels on a global risk-off event
picks.keyRisk: Fed Chair Warsh executes a rapid series of hikes that definitively breaks inflation by mid-2027, sending real yields to +2%+ and structurally removing gold's rate-differential appeal

picks.name: Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF
picks.rationale: SCHD provides systematic exposure to quality compounders screened on FCF yield, dividend growth, and balance sheet strength — delivering 3.5%+ dividend yield with overweights in Financials, Consumer Staples, and Healthcare, exactly the late-cycle defensive rotation sectors identified as leading since June 5. It systematically excludes high-multiple AI names (zero NVDA, TSLA, PLTR), providing a built-in hedge against the AI capex reset tail risk that could re-rate the growth book. In a higher-for-longer rate environment, the income component provides real return buffer.
picks.catalyst: Late-cycle defensive rotation accelerates into August if Warsh signals hike bias at July 28-29 FOMC; quarterly rebalance adds value via factor discipline
picks.catalystWindow: Ongoing; FOMC July 28-29 as near-term trigger
picks.entryNote: SCHD offers 3.5%+ current yield at a time when the earnings yield on SPY is 3.93% — near parity, making income-generating defensives structurally competitive vs. growth. No single-name concentration risk. Low beta historically in risk-off tapes.
picks.exitTrigger: Fed definitively pivots to rate cuts, risk appetite broadens dramatically, and growth-over-value factor spread widens more than 20% — reducing the defensive diversification value
picks.keyRisk: A sustained growth reacceleration with falling inflation (soft landing) makes defensive dividend payers a persistent opportunity cost vs. high-momentum growth names

picks.name: Berkshire Hathaway Inc. Class B
picks.rationale: Berkshire ended Q1 2026 with a record $397.4B cash pile — ~59% of its investable portfolio in Treasury Bills — signaling Greg Abel does not see broad value at current market levels, making BRK.B a de facto low-volatility money-market alternative with equity upside via GEICO, BNSF, and energy operating businesses. Abel's largest single bet is the ~$16.6B GOOGL position (already held in our growth book), confirming alignment of conviction. BRK.B historically exhibits 0.6-0.7 beta in risk-off tapes with no tech/antitrust regulatory exposure.

picks.entryNote: Trades at ~1.5x book — not cheap historically, but rational for a $400B+ cash-generating conglomerate with permanent capital structure. The $397B cash fortress represents extraordinary optionality for a crisis acquisition that could re-rate shares +20-30% instantly.
picks.exitTrigger: Abel begins deploying the cash reserve aggressively into overvalued assets, signaling the capital discipline that underlies the defensive thesis has eroded; or catastrophic insurance losses impair book value
picks.keyRisk: Berkshire's massive Apple position (~22% of equity portfolio) creates single-stock concentration that would impair NAV if AAPL de-rates due to iPhone unit cycle weakness or AI monetization disappointment

picks.name: iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF
picks.rationale: IEF provides intermediate-duration Treasury exposure at the 7-10 year part of the curve where the 10-year yield currently sits at 4.57% — near a multi-month high and offering the highest real yield available to long-only equity portfolios as a risk-off shock absorber. The 2s10s at +41bp and the 3m10y at +78bp confirm a cautious growth backdrop; any risk-off event (Hormuz re-escalation, Fed hike shock) would bid the belly of the curve and produce positive returns in IEF that partially offset growth book drawdowns. Sized modestly at 4% as the equity risk premium is barely positive and the Fed could still hike.

picks.entryNote: 10-year yield at 4.57% — highest since May and a multi-year entry point by historical standards. Duration ~7.5 years; a 50bp rally in yields produces ~3.75% price appreciation. Not a rate bet — a portfolio hedge. Sized at 4% medium conviction consistent with prior positioning.

picks.keyRisk: Fed resumes hiking cycle and 10-year yields continue rising toward 5%+, causing IEF to deliver negative returns and fail its portfolio hedge function simultaneously with equity stress

picks.name: The Coca-Cola Company
picks.rationale: KO is the portfolio's Consumer Staples ballast — a dividend-growth compounder with genuine pricing power in an inflationary environment, a Berkshire Hathaway top-3 holding providing conviction overlap, and a business that has grown organically through every recession since WWII. Consumer Staples led sector performance since June 5 (+3.8%), reflecting the late-cycle rotation signal from the sector scorecard. In a stagflationary regime where shelter and food prices remain elevated, KO's ability to pass costs through via brand equity insulates margins and supports the dividend (60+ consecutive years of growth).
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — organic revenue growth and pricing realization in international markets amid USD strength
picks.entryNote: KO trades at ~22x forward earnings — modest premium to Staples sector average, justified by dividend growth history and global brand optionality. Dividend yield ~3%+ provides real income in a higher-for-longer environment. Technically stable above 200-day SMA.

weeklyReport.summary: The portfolio operates in a late-cycle stagflationary regime — the Fed holds at 3.50-3.75% with a hawkish bias, core PCE sits at 3.3%, the 10-year is at 4.57%, and the equity risk premium is barely positive at the forward earnings level — demanding a Cautiously Bullish posture with a defensiveScore of 4. The ten-name growth book concentrates conviction in AI infrastructure compounders (AVGO, GOOGL, META, CRWD, MU) that are delivering fundamental earnings beats far above consensus, complemented by diversifying positions in Financials (GS, SPGI), Industrials/Defense (RTX, PWR), and Power Infrastructure (VST) that are direct beneficiaries of the physical AI buildout and defense ramp. The defensive sleeve (GLD, SCHD, BRK.B, IEF, KO) hedges three distinct tail risks: stagflation re-escalation via the Hormuz ceasefire fragility (GLD), AI capex reset and multiple compression (SCHD/BRK.B cash optionality), and Treasury yield spike into FOMC (IEF). All core holdings from the past seven days carry over — AVGO, GOOGL, GS, META, SPGI, RTX, PWR, VST, CRWD, GLD, SCHD, BRK.B, IEF, KO — with MU retained at reduced weight as a 'fallen angel' accumulation thesis replacing the prior ISRG slot given MU's stronger institutional smart-money backing (Tepper +6x) and HBM structural take-or-pay anchors. The dominant risk being underwritten is an October 2026 Fed hike materializing alongside a Hormuz re-escalation that simultaneously compresses multiples, re-ignites headline inflation, and triggers an AI capex pause — a compound event that the defensive sleeve provides partial but not complete protection against.
weeklyReport.diversificationNote: The growth book spans five GICS sectors: Information Technology (AVGO, CRWD, MU — three names, at the maximum allowable concentration), Communication Services (GOOGL, META), Financials (GS, SPGI), Industrials (RTX, PWR), and Utilities (VST); AVGO and MU share AI semiconductor exposure but occupy different value chain positions (custom ASIC/networking vs. HBM memory), while CRWD addresses the cybersecurity layer; the defensive sleeve spans four distinct exposures — gold commodity hedge (GLD), broad dividend/quality income ETF (SCHD), all-weather operating conglomerate with cash optionality (BRK.B), intermediate Treasury duration (IEF), and Consumer Staples dividend compounder (KO) — with no exposure overlap across the five defensive picks.

phaseData.macro:
Here is your full macro briefing as of Friday, July 17, 2026:

---

## 📊 US EQUITIES MACRO BRIEFING — July 17, 2026

---

### DATA DASHBOARD

**Fed Policy**

New Fed Chair Kevin Warsh held the target range at **3.50%–3.75%** at the June 17 FOMC meeting.
 
The updated dot plot reflects a hawkish pivot, with a **3.8% median rate projection for 2026**, signaling fewer expected rate cuts.
 
Markets are currently pricing in **one 25 bp hike by October 2026**, with no further movement through 2027; Fed officials raised year-end rate projections to between **3.6% and 4.1%**.
 
The longer-run neutral rate anchor sits at **3.0%
**, placing the real policy rate (effective FFR ~3.63% minus ~3.3% core PCE) around +30 bp — modestly restrictive but far tighter than the neutral rate implies given still-elevated inflation.

**Yield Curve**

As of July 16, the curve is upward-sloping: **2Y at 4.16%**, **5Y at 4.28%**, **10Y at 4.57%**, and **30Y at 5.09%**.
 
The **2s10s spread is +41 bp** and the **3m10y spread is +78 bp**.
 
Positive long-short spreads are consistent with a cautious growth outlook and persistent inflation; elevated term premia and steady Treasury issuance continue to support a "higher for longer" yield regime.


**Inflation**

**June CPI printed 3.5% YoY**, below the 3.8% forecast, easing inflation fears even as oil surged past $80 and Iran tensions escalated.
 
**Core CPI was flat month-over-month**, with the 12-month rate easing sharply to **2.6%**, against a consensus of ~2.9%.
 
The energy index fell 5.7% on the month, reflecting a sharp drop in fuel prices following a US-Iran ceasefire that eased Strait of Hormuz supply concerns.
 The most recent PCE data (April): 
PCE rose **3.8% YoY**, with **core PCE at 3.3%**, indicating underlying inflation pressures remain elevated.
 
The Fed significantly raised its 2026 PCE forecast to **3.6% headline** and **3.3% core** in the June SEP.
 Trajectory: **decelerating at the headline** (energy-led), but **core/supercore remains sticky** well above the 2% target.

**Growth**

The June SEP projects **real GDP growth of 2.2% in 2026**, 2.3% in 2027, and 2.2% in 2028.
 
The labor market refuses to soften: May payrolls came in at **+172K** vs. an +80K consensus, with revisions adding +93K to the prior two months, and unemployment holding at **4.3%**.
 
Economic activity is described as "expanding at a solid pace" with productivity growth and capital investment strong.


**Credit & Liquidity**

The S&P 500 closed July 10 near a record at **7,575**, with the VIX at a low **15.03**, signaling benign financial conditions despite geopolitical noise.
 
The 10-year Treasury yield is around **4.56–4.57%**, its highest since May, with the FOMC at 3.50–3.75% and nine officials signaling a possible rate rise by year-end.
 (Note: precise HY OAS and IG OAS intraday data not returned in searches; the low VIX and near-record equity levels are consistent with tight spread conditions.) 
PPI for final demand **fell 0.3% in June** — goods prices down 1.4%, services up 0.2% — though PPI remains up **5.5% YoY**, keeping pipeline inflation alive.


**Earnings Backdrop**

The **S&P 500 trailing earnings yield is 3.93%**, about 2.7% below its long-term average of 4.04%.
 
The realized earnings yield of ~3.4% sits below the 10-year yield near 4.5%, leaving the equity risk premium at roughly **–110 bp**, the widest negative reading since 2003.
 
Using forward P/E, the earnings yield rises to ~4.5%, slightly above the 10-year — "bonds are winning on realized earnings, stocks still have a tiny edge if earnings keep showing up."
 
S&P 500 EPS revisions have actually accelerated since the start of the Iran conflict, with Technology estimates revised sharply higher since mid-February.


**Geopolitical / Event Tape**

US and Iranian forces exchanged strikes in mid-July; Iran declared the Strait of Hormuz closed while US Central Command said transits continue — roughly a fifth to a quarter of the world's seaborne oil normally passes through it.
 
A partial ceasefire MOU eased the immediate supply shock, driving energy prices lower in June.
 
However, as of July 16, the 10-year yield climbed back to 4.57% amid rising oil prices reigniting inflation fears and a strained ceasefire.


---

### 🔴 PARAGRAPH 1 — REGIME CALL

**Late-cycle, with a stagflationary undertow.** The regime is unambiguously late-cycle: 
the Fed is on hold at 3.50–3.75% with a hawkish pivot to a 3.8% median rate projection — signaling a prolonged restrictive stance
 — while 
inflation has remained above the 2% target for more than five years, with headline inflation driven higher this year by the Iran war and energy shock.
 The nominal policy rate sits meaningfully above the long-run neutral of 3.0%, the yield curve has only just re-steepened from deep inversion (not a signal of recovery but of term premium repricing), 
the equity risk premium is the most negative since 2003,
 and growth is holding but decelerating from peak. Credit is still tightly priced, but the margin for error is thin — one sustained energy re-shock or a hike materializing would tip the balance into contraction.

---

### 🟡 PARAGRAPH 2 — WHAT'S WORKING / WHAT'S BREAKING

**What's working:** 
Market leadership has stayed concentrated in AI-linked names, with the S&P 500 near record levels and the Nasdaq at 26,281; a cautious tone from ASML or TSMC could weigh more widely, while a confident one could extend the theme.
 
Technology EPS estimates have been revised sharply higher since mid-February, with names like Oracle and Micron leading AI infrastructure spending.
 Energy has also benefited from the oil shock, though that's now partially reversing on the ceasefire. Quality/large-cap growth, AI capex beneficiaries (semiconductors, power infrastructure, data centers), and Energy remain the working factors. **What's breaking:** Rate-sensitive sectors — REITs, small caps, utilities — are structurally challenged with the 30-year at **5.09%** and nine FOMC officials signaling a potential hike. 
Shelter and food prices remain above pre-shock levels even as energy retreats,
 compressing consumer discretionary margins and pressuring lower-income cohorts. Industrials and transports face dual headwinds: a potential Hormuz disruption in supply chains plus cost-of-capital pressure. 
IBM cratered 22% on a Q2 miss as clients shifted capex toward hardware/memory rather than software services
 — a warning that the AI spending rotation is cannibalizing enterprise software budgets.

---

### 🟠 PARAGRAPH 3 — WHAT TO WATCH (Top 2–3 Catalysts, Next 1–6 Months)

1. **July 28–29 FOMC Meeting & Chair Warsh's Press Conference.** 
Positive long-short spreads are consistent with the Fed holding policy steady at the July meeting
, but 
markets price one 25 bp hike by October 2026.
 Warsh's tone on whether the June CPI break is durable — or energy-transient — is the single most important risk on/off toggle for equity multiples. A confirmed hike bias sends the 2Y toward 4.5%+ and compresses the forward ERP to outright negative territory.

2. **Strait of Hormuz / Iran Ceasefire Durability.** 
Iran declared the strait closed while US Central Command said transits continue
; any breakdown of the fragile MOU would immediately reprice oil, re-accelerate headline CPI, and force the Fed's hand. 
The conflict has already driven energy prices up 23.5% YoY
 — a second leg higher would eliminate the June CPI disinflation print and revive hike pricing, likely cracking the tech-led equity narrative.

3. **Q2 Earnings Season Guidance (July–August).** 
The S&P 500 is near 7,545 with the Nasdaq leading on the June CPI surprise,
 but the rally rests almost entirely on forward EPS delivery. 
The forward earnings yield barely exceeds the 10-year yield; if the 10-year definitively breaks above 4.6% and surges, the investment math shifts strongly in favor of bonds over stocks.
 Watch margin guidance from mega-cap tech (NVDA, MSFT, GOOGL reporting late July/early August) — any demand softness or capex pull-forward warnings could reprice the entire AI multiple premium that holds the index up.

# 🏦 SECTOR ROTATION MASTER RANKING — JULY 17, 2026
### Long-Only Fund | 1–5 Year Horizon | Ranked Top → Bottom

The first half of 2026 has been marked by moderate GDP growth, persistent inflationary pressures, and shifting interest rate expectations.
 
Fed Chair Warsh's hawkish debut — the "price stability comes first" message — repriced the market away from rate cuts and hit long-duration growth leaders hardest in June.
 
The index-level forward P/E compressed from 21.89x to ~20.32x, back to the 10-year average, improving the equity risk premium from a razor-thin 0.10pp to 0.43pp.

 This is a **mid-cycle broadening** — leadership is rotating from hyper-concentrated mega-cap tech into quality cyclicals and defensives with real earnings.

## 3. 🌪️ MACRO TAIL RISKS — 12-Month Horizon

### Tail Risk #1: **Fed Hike Cycle Resumes — "Stagflation Lock-In"**
**Probability Tier: ELEVATED**


On June 17, 2026, the Federal Reserve held its benchmark rate steady at 3.50–3.75% in Kevin Warsh's first meeting as Fed Chair — but the accompanying signals were notably hawkish: nine of eighteen committee members now project a rate hike before year-end, the median dot-plot projection for year-end 2026 jumped to 3.8% from 3.4% in March, and the post-meeting statement removed language signaling any future easing bias.
 
The Committee raised its 2026 Core PCE projection from 2.7% in March to 3.3% in June, a sixty-basis-point upgrade in one quarter. Headline PCE went from 2.7% to 3.6%, a ninety-basis-point upgrade. Real GDP for 2026 was cut to 2.2%, and the projected return to the 2% target was pushed out to 2028.
 
Investors are underestimating how determined Federal Reserve Chairman Kevin Warsh is to bring inflation back to the central bank's 2% target and the likely drag that will create on risk assets, according to Citadel Securities.


**Trigger**: One more hot CPI print (≥4.5% YoY) or a re-escalation in Hormuz energy prices forces Warsh to hike 25bps at the September meeting.

**Sectors hurt most**: Long-duration tech (NVDA, PLTR, ARM, MSFT), REITs (EQIX, PLD, AMT), Utilities (NEE, D, DUK), TLT, IEF. Growth stocks with no current earnings hit hardest.

---

### Tail Risk #2: **AI Capex Reset / Hyperscaler Spending Pullback**
**Probability Tier: MEDIUM**


What derails all four AI momentum names together is an AI capex reset.
 Microsoft, Google, Amazon, and Meta have collectively committed to $300B+ in AI infrastructure capex in 2026. If any two of the "Big Four" hyperscalers signal capex moderation on Q2/Q3 earnings calls — citing ROI concerns, model efficiency improvements (e.g., a second DeepSeek-style moment), or slowing enterprise AI adoption — the entire semiconductor and AI infrastructure complex re-rates simultaneously. 
Nvidia, AMD, Broadcom, Micron, Intel, Applied Materials, Lam Research, and KLA have all already traded under pressure as investors reassessed whether AI infrastructure spending remains a durable growth driver or has become a valuation risk.


**Trigger**: One hyperscaler (likely MSFT or GOOGL) guides AI infrastructure capex flat-to-down on a Q3 2026 earnings call; or a breakthrough in model efficiency renders current-generation GPU demand structurally lower than expected.

**Sectors hurt most**: NVDA (-30–40%), AMD (-25–35%), AVGO, MRVL, ANET, MU, TSM, ALAB. Spillover into MSFT and GOOGL multiples.

---

### Tail Risk #3: **Strait of Hormuz Re-Escalation / Oil Spike to $110+**
**Probability Tier: MEDIUM**


The US-Iran Strait of Hormuz de-escalation remains incomplete: the 60-day ceasefire framework (signed June 17) continues to be tested — Iran briefly re-closed the strait on June 20, citing Israeli strikes in Lebanon, before a US Navy-backed widened shipping route near Oman was announced June 27.
 
The cause of May's CPI surge to 4.2% year-on-year was driven largely by Hormuz-related energy costs.
 A full closure of the Strait — through which ~20% of global oil passes — would spike WTI above $100–$110 and re-ignite the stagflation scenario simultaneously with Tail Risk #1.

**Trigger**: Israeli strikes on Iranian nuclear facilities; Iranian Revolutionary Guard mining of shipping lanes; breakdown of the June 17 ceasefire framework.

**Sectors hurt most**: Airlines (not in universe), consumer discretionary (AMZN, TGT, LOW, SBUX via margin compression), Autos/EV (TSLA), and paradoxically benefits XOM, CVX, OXY, COP. TLT sells off (inflation), credit spreads widen. The Warsh Fed is forced into a corner — hike into a supply shock.

## 📊 US Equity Regime Briefing | July 20, 2026

---

### DATA DASHBOARD

**FED POLICY**

The June 16–17 FOMC meeting concluded with a unanimous hold at 3.50%–3.75%, but the tone turned decisively hawkish. The updated dot plot raised the median year-end 2026 federal funds rate projection to 3.8% — up from 3.4% in March — signaling no cuts this year and leaving the door open to another hike.
 
New Chair Kevin Warsh, presiding over his first FOMC decision, stated that inflation "remains elevated" relative to the 2% goal.
 
The Fed's own June dot plot shows the median policymaker now expects rates to end 2026 higher than today, with 17 of 18 officials seeing inflation risk on the upside.
 
Based on current futures pricing, the most likely outcome is one 25 bp cut bringing the target range to 3.25–3.50% by year-end, with ~55–65% of market participants favoring September or October timing. There is a meaningful 30–40% probability the Fed doesn't cut at all in 2026.
 Real policy rate is meaningfully positive: with the funds rate at 3.625% (midpoint) and core CPI at 2.6%, the real rate is ~+100 bp, above most estimates of neutral (~0.5–1.0% real), confirming policy is restrictive.

**YIELD CURVE**

The 10-year yield sits at roughly 4.18%.
 
The front end (3-month bills) sits roughly in line with the policy rate at 4.25%, while the long end has rallied below 4.20% on a 10-year basis.
 
The 2s10s spread is positive but narrow, while the 3m10y has only just crossed below zero — a combination that is a classic late-cycle signature.
 
The 3m10y spread, used by the NY Fed in its recession-probability model, has flipped negative again after a brief positive excursion in early 2026. The 2s10s, which inverted continuously from mid-2022 to late-2024, is now narrowly positive but well below the 100–150 bp range typical of a healthy expansion.
 
The very long end (20–30Y) sits modestly above the 10Y, restoring a small steepness in the back of the curve — a feature absent during 2022–2024.
 
The term premium on the 10-year is approximately 50–60 bp, low by historical standards, reflecting strong demand from pension funds and foreign reserve managers.


**INFLATION**

The June CPI report (released July 14) showed headline inflation at 3.5% YoY and core CPI at 2.6% YoY — still well above the Fed's 2% goal.
 
That headline print is down sharply from May's 4.2%, representing the first clear sequential disinflation signal in months.
 
However, core PCE remains sticky at 3.3%.
 
The June FOMC minutes flagged persistent price pressures from tariffs, AI-related investment, and geopolitical tensions, with staff nudging inflation projections higher for both 2026 and 2027.
 Trajectory: **decelerating from May's spike but sticky well above target** — one month of disinflation does not yet constitute a trend in the Committee's view.

**GROWTH**

The updated June SEP sees GDP growth at 2.2% for 2026.
 
The labor market is cooling: June nonfarm payrolls rose just 57,000 — a sharp slowdown from May's downwardly revised 129,000 — while unemployment edged down to 4.2% and average hourly earnings rose 3.5% YoY.
 
June ADP private payrolls came in at just 98,000, below the ~120,000 expected.
 ISM data and GDPNow were not captured in today's search results with a precise July read, but the payrolls run-rate of ~57K–129K over the last two months signals a sub-trend labor market consistent with late-cycle deceleration.

**CREDIT & LIQUIDITY**

HY OAS (ICE BofA) stands at 269 bps as of July 10, 2026.
 
BBB (IG) option-adjusted spreads were approximately 100 bps in April 2026, near 25-year tights.
 HY OAS at 269 bp is historically tight but has widened modestly from the March/April lows, consistent with a market beginning to price in late-cycle credit risk without yet panicking. Financial conditions indexes (Goldman GSFCI, Chicago Fed NFCI) remain broadly accommodative given tight credit spreads and equity resilience, though the DXY has been pressured by the rate-path uncertainty.

**EARNINGS BACKDROP**

S&P 500 EPS revisions have actually accelerated over the last several weeks (post-Iran conflict onset), notably driven by sharply higher technology estimates — a big chunk attributed to Oracle and Micron.
 
The current S&P 500 trailing earnings yield is approximately 3.12%
, implying a trailing P/E near **32x** — well above the 10-year average of ~19–21x. Forward P/E is estimated at roughly **22–23x** on consensus forward EPS of ~$270–280 (2026E), also above the 10-year average of ~18x, offering a thin equity risk premium versus a 4.18% 10-year yield.

**GEOPOLITICAL / EVENT TAPE**

The ongoing conflict in the Middle East has added geopolitical uncertainty to the macro backdrop.
 
Factors including the long-term potential impacts of the Iran War, tariff disagreements, and AI-driven labor market disruption are material uncertainty variables for the next 1–6 months.


---

### ¶1 — REGIME CALL

**Late-cycle / transitioning toward contraction.** The macro configuration checks nearly every late-cycle box: 
the yield curve has flattened from its prior inversion but has not restored a healthy upward slope — the 3m10y has just crossed below zero, the 2s10s is narrowly positive — a classic late-cycle signature.
 
Payroll creation has collapsed to just 57,000 in June
, real policy rates are positive and restrictive at ~+100 bp above core CPI, 
inflation remains sticky with tariff-driven upside risks acknowledged by the Fed itself
, and 
17 of 18 FOMC members see inflation risks skewed to the upside with the dot plot pointing to higher, not lower, rates by year-end.
 This is a stagflationary late-cycle, not a soft-landing mid-cycle: growth is decelerating faster than inflation, compressing the Fed's ability to respond.

---

### ¶2 — WHAT'S WORKING / WHAT'S BREAKING

**Working:** Quality and cash-flow durability are the dominant factor — large-cap technology is being rescued by AI capex-driven EPS upgrades (
technology estimates have been revised sharply higher since mid-February 2026
), making mega-cap tech (NVDA, ORCL, MU supply chain) the exception to the late-cycle rule. Energy names have begun to see estimate boosts given the Middle East risk premium. Short-duration value (Financials collecting on the wide net interest margin afforded by a 3.50%–3.75% funds rate), Healthcare (defensive earnings, non-cyclical demand), and Utilities (pricing power + rate-sensitivity mean-reversion if the Fed eventually pivots) are tactically attractive. Credit-sensitive shorts are building a case — HY at 269 bp OAS is historically tight for a world where payrolls just printed 57K; any credit re-pricing would hit Consumer Discretionary, Small Caps (IWM), and leveraged-buyout-heavy sectors hard. **Breaking:** Rate-sensitive growth (unprofitable tech, biotech, long-duration SPAC residuals) faces a double-squeeze of high discount rates and margin pressure. 
REITs face higher borrowing costs, adding pressure on profitability.
 Consumer Discretionary is vulnerable as 
autos, durable goods, and apparel historically underperform when people defer discretionary spending
 under persistent inflation and high rates. Small-cap equities face the most direct earnings risk from a cooling labor market and tight bank lending standards.

---

### ¶3 — WHAT TO WATCH (Highest-Impact, Next 1–6 Months)

1. **July 28–29 FOMC Decision + Press Conference:** 
Markets currently price a hold at 3.50%–3.75% as the most likely outcome, though the updated dot plot now points to a possible hike later in 2026 rather than a cut.
 Any shift in language toward a hike, or a hawkish Warsh press conference, would be a material negative catalyst for both equities (via multiple compression) and credit (HY OAS re-widening). This is the single highest-impact near-term event.

2. **June PCE (July 30) + Subsequent CPI Prints:** 
The June PCE report lands July 30, the morning after the FOMC decision
 — a rare back-to-back risk event. 
Core PCE currently sits at 3.3%
; a print above that reignites hike speculation and breaks the nascent disinflation narrative from the June CPI. Conversely, a sub-3.0% core PCE would be the single largest positive catalyst for a re-rating of equities and could price in the September cut market participants are hoping for.

3. **Iran War / Middle East Escalation & Tariff Tape:** 
The long-term impacts of the Iran conflict and tariff disagreements remain live uncertainty variables.
 An oil price spike above $100/bbl (Brent) from Middle East escalation would re-accelerate headline CPI, close the door on any Fed cuts, and deliver a stagflationary shock to consumer sectors. Simultaneously, any material tariff escalation or de-escalation with major trading partners will move forward EPS estimates significantly, given how heavily technology and industrial supply chains are exposed. Watch the geopolitical tape weekly — this is the tail risk with the highest non-linear impact on the 3–6 month horizon.
